title: Research Methodology
text:
Randomised controlled experiment. Individual-level randomisation to nudge vs control condition within structured lab sessions across three or more university units spanning Information Systems, Supply Chain Management, and Marketing.
All sessions use a frontier-class AI model (Claude or GPT-4 class) accessed via API. This is a deliberate design choice: to isolate the effect of the nudge, the underlying model must be capable enough that variation in output quality reflects student interaction rather than model limitations.

title: Research Methodology
text:
Randomised controlled experiment. Individual-level randomisation to nudge vs control condition within structured lab sessions across three or more university units spanning Information Systems, Supply Chain Management, and Marketing.
All sessions use a frontier-class AI model (Claude or GPT-4 class) accessed via API. This is a deliberate design choice: to isolate the effect of the nudge, the underlying model must be capable enough that variation in output quality reflects student interaction rather than model limitations.

title: Research Methodology
text:
Participants and Recruitment
Population: Curtin students enrolled in participating units (ISYS6020 + at least two additional units). Participation is voluntary and does not affect grades.
Target sample: ~60-120 participants total (20-30 per session across 3+ units). A power analysis will be finalised once units and cohort sizes are confirmed.
Recruitment: Via PI and co-investigators with plain-language invitations on LMS and email. Session timing varied to accommodate students with work or caring commitments. Purposive encouragement of participation from low SES and first-in-family students.
Inclusion: Enrolled students in participating units; aged 18+; able to attend scheduled lab sessions.
Exclusion: Students under 18; students who do not consent; students without capacity to consent.

title: Research Methodology
text:
The Intervention

Session Format
Each session follows a consistent format (45-60 minutes):

Student logs in with student number (assigned a random session code)
Student is randomly assigned to nudge or control condition
Student completes a discipline-relevant task using the AI chat interface
Conversations are logged automatically throughout
10-minute exit survey via Qualtrics on completion

The AI interface is provided and configured by the research team. Students use lab computers; no personal account or device is required.


The Conversational Nudge
In the nudge condition, a short prompt is appended to every AI response. To reduce habituation, the prompt is randomly selected each turn from a pool of 10 variants. All variants follow the same design principles:

Invite pushback, not confirmation
Domain-agnostic (work across all disciplines)
No prior AI experience assumed
Make the conversational path easier, not mandatory

Example variants:

“Does that match what you expected? If something seems off, tell me what seems wrong.”
“What’s the weakest part of that response?”
“Does that account for the details in your task, or is it a generic answer?”

The nudge is appended server-side after the AI response. The AI model never sees the nudge text - it is not included in the conversation history sent to the API. This ensures the AI’s behaviour is identical across conditions.
See Nudge Variants for the full pool.


System Prompt
A system prompt instructs the AI model to answer directly without follow-up suggestions, engagement hooks, or offers to elaborate. This prevents the model’s default behaviour from contaminating the control condition (frontier models are trained to keep users engaged, which would create pseudo-nudges in both conditions).


Task Briefs
Each discipline has a primary conversational task requiring iterative dialogue to develop a recommendation or analysis:

Information Systems - AI demand forecasting recommendation
Supply Chain Management - supply chain resilience briefing
Marketing - retail channel strategy assessment

Retrieval-framed task variants are designed for a planned follow-on study.

title: Research Methodology
text:
Measures

Automated Measures
Computed directly from transcript logs via script. No human coding required.







Measure
Definition




turns_total
Total student turns


turns_coded
Turns coded on behavioural dimensions (Turn 2+)


words_mean_student
Average words per student turn


question_marks_proportion
Proportion of turns with a question mark


duration_minutes
Session duration


response_latency_mean
Mean time between AI response and student reply





Human-Coded Behavioural Dimensions
The RA codes 100% of transcripts from Turn 2 onward (Turn 1 is excluded as it cannot be a follow-up, challenge, or extension). Three binary dimensions per turn:








Dimension
Code
Definition




Follow-Up (FU)
0/1
Did the student ask a question that continues the conversation?


Challenge (CH)
0/1
Did the student push back on, doubt, or correct the AI?


Extension (EX)
0/1
Did the student introduce new information or a new angle?



Delegation is derived in analysis (FU=0, CH=0, EX=0), not coded separately. This eliminates the possibility of contradictory data.


Turn-Level Scoring (Dual)









Score
Formula
Range
Rationale




Unweighted
FU + CH + EX
0-3
Treats all dimensions equally


Challenge-weighted
FU + 2xCH + EX
0-4
Emphasises Challenge, the dimension most directly targeted by the nudge



Both scores are reported. If they converge, the finding is robust to scoring assumptions. If they diverge, it reveals whether the nudge primarily drives challenge behaviour or broader engagement.


Task Completion Score
Scored by co-investigators on their own unit’s transcripts. Scale 1-5 (minimal output to comprehensive analysis). Not comparable across units - discipline-specific rubric anchors.


Exit Survey
Administered via Qualtrics. Captures:

Confidence calibration (Q2 vs task score - within-unit only)
Self-reported vs observed interaction style (Q5/Q6 vs coded transcript)
Condition awareness (Q8a - did nudge students notice the nudge?)
Prior AI experience (Q9/Q10 - baseline covariate)

See Exit Survey for the full instrument.

title: Research Methodology
text:
Data Collection and De-identification

Student enters student number at session login. System assigns a random session code and records a temporary linkage.
All research data (transcripts, task outputs, survey) tagged with session code only.
After data collection closes, the linkage table is used to match equity flags (low SES, first-in-family) from Curtin SIMS.
A de-identification script strips student numbers. The linkage table is permanently destroyed. Destruction is logged and auditable.
The research team analyses a fully de-identified dataset.

title: Research Methodology
text:
Analysis Plan

Primary Analysis
Linear mixed-effects model accounting for the nested structure (students within units):
outcome ~ condition + (1 | unit)

outcome: session-level measure (e.g., mean_turn_score_raw, proportion_challenge)
condition: fixed effect (Nudge vs Control)
(1 | unit): random intercept for unit

Why mixed-effects: Students are not independent across units. Each unit has a different task, discipline, and facilitator. Simple ANOVA ignores this nesting and underestimates standard errors.
Covariate: Prior AI experience (Q10) included as a fixed effect if groups differ at baseline.


Equity-Disaggregated Analysis
outcome ~ condition * equity_flag + (1 | unit)
A significant interaction indicates the nudge effect differs by equity cohort (differential benefit). A non-significant interaction with a significant main effect indicates uniform benefit. Both findings are reported.


Confidence Calibration
Conducted within unit only (task scores not comparable across disciplines):

Spearman correlation between self-reported confidence and task score
Comparison of the confidence-performance gap between conditions
Identification of the “overconfident delegator” pattern



Inter-Rater Reliability

RA codes 100% of transcripts on FU, CH, EX
Each co-investigator independently codes a 20-30% stratified sample from their unit
Cohen’s kappa calculated per unit
Kappa < 0.70 triggers scheme revision before full analysis

See Coding Scheme for the complete specification.


Reporting
All analyses reported with:

Effect sizes (Cohen’s d; marginal R-squared for mixed models)
95% confidence intervals
Both unweighted and weighted scoring results
Equity-disaggregated results regardless of significance

Implementation: R with lme4, or Python with statsmodels.

title: Research Methodology
text:
Ethical Considerations
HREC application submitted under Curtin’s low-risk review pathway. See the Ethics Package for the full application and supporting documents.
Key protections:

Student numbers collected at login for equity linkage only; destroyed after de-identification
All research data uses session codes (no names, no student IDs)
Participation voluntary, does not affect grades
No-penalty alternative activity available
Withdrawal possible until de-identification
Nudge transparently described in PIS (no deception)

title: Data Management Plan
text:
Data types: AI chat transcripts (session ID), task outputs, exit survey responses, and equity flags (low SES; first-in-family) added by Curtin analytics and delivered de-identified. No PII collected by research team.
Storage: Curtin SharePoint/Network drive (restricted access, encryption at rest). Access limited to PI, named co-Is, and RA.
Processing: RA codes 100% of transcripts; co-Is code 20-30% calibration per unit; Cohen’s kappa computed; kappa < 0.70 triggers scheme refinement.
Sharing: De-identified aggregates and anonymised excerpts only; no raw transcripts externalised.
Retention and destruction: Retain 5 years post-publication; then secure deletion. Backups per Curtin IT policy.

title: HREC Narrative
text:
Project Title: Closing the AI Literacy Gap: Scaffolding Equitable Participation in AI-Integrated Assessment
Principal Investigator: Michael Borck, Lecturer, School of Marketing and Management (SoMM)
Co-Investigators:
Participating Units:

title: HREC Narrative
text:
Plain Language Summary
This study evaluates whether a simple conversational nudge improves how students interact with AI during structured lab sessions. Students complete a short, discipline-relevant task using a supplied AI chat tool. Half of students (randomised) see a one-line reminder appended to the AI’s replies: “Does that match what you expected? If something seems off, tell me what seems wrong.” The other half do not. We compare interaction quality, task outcomes, and confidence. Student numbers are collected at session login for equity flag linkage only; a de-identification script removes them after matching, and the linkage table is permanently destroyed. The research team analyses only de-identified data (session codes and equity flags). Participation is voluntary, does not affect grades, and students may withdraw at any time before data aggregation. Findings will support equitable AI-integrated assessment at Curtin.

title: HREC Narrative
text:
Aims and Significance

Determine whether a minimal conversational nudge increases critical, iterative AI interaction.
Examine whether benefits are uniform or differential for low SES and first-in-family students using de-identified matched data.

Significance: Provides low-cost, scalable in-class scaffolding to address structural disparities in AI literacy and supports Curtin’s NBF reporting.

title: HREC Narrative
text:
Methods
Design: Randomised controlled lab sessions within 3 or more units; assignment to Nudge vs Control. All sessions use a frontier-class AI model via API.
Setting and Procedure: 45-60 minute on-campus sessions using Curtin lab computers; iterative AI task; automatic transcript logging (session IDs); 10-minute digital exit survey.
Recruitment: LMS/email invitations with plain-language PIS; participation voluntary; alternative activity available; no effect on grades.
Participants: Enrolled students (18+) in participating units; exclude under-18s and non-consenting students.
Sample: ~60-120 students total; power analysis to be finalised post unit confirmations.
Data: Student numbers collected at login for equity linkage only (destroyed after de-identification). AI transcripts (session code), task outputs, Qualtrics exit survey responses. Equity flags matched via temporary linkage table, then de-identified.
Analysis: Linear mixed-effects models (condition as fixed effect, unit as random intercept); effect sizes; equity-disaggregated subgroup analysis with interaction terms; confidence calibration within-unit only. RA codes 100% of transcripts (Turn 2 onward); co-Is code 20-30% per unit; Cohen’s kappa with kappa < 0.70 triggering refinement.

title: HREC Narrative
text:
Risks and Mitigation (Low-Risk Justification)
Physical risks: none. Psychological discomfort: minimal; mitigations include voluntariness, option to pause/withdraw, supportive facilitation. Power dynamics: neutral recruitment, alternatives to participation. Privacy: student numbers collected at login for equity linkage only; stored in a restricted temporary linkage table; destroyed after de-identification script runs. Research data uses session codes only. Data stored on Curtin systems with restricted access.

title: HREC Narrative
text:
Consent (Digital Only)
Digital consent via Qualtrics (Curtin institutional licence) prior to sessions. No deception. Withdrawal permitted any time before data aggregation using session codes.

title: HREC Narrative
text:
Privacy, Confidentiality and Data Security
Student numbers held in temporary linkage table only; destroyed after equity flag matching. All research data uses session codes. Storage on Curtin network/SharePoint with role-based access and encryption at rest. Retention: 5 years post-publication; secure deletion thereafter. No raw transcripts shared externally.

title: HREC Narrative
text:
Use of AI / External Services
Frontier model via API: prompts/responses transmitted without PII (session codes only); provider training opt-out enabled where available.

title: HREC Narrative
text:
Funding, Conflicts, Monitoring and Timeline
Funding: NBF Learning & Teaching Grant 2026 (AUD $20,000). No conflicts declared. Monitoring: PI reviews logs after sessions; adverse events reported to HREC. Timeline: Apr-Dec 2026 data collection and dissemination; reporting in 2027.

title: Task Brief - Information Systems
text: You are an IT consultant advising a mid-sized Australian retail company (250 employees, 12 stores, one warehouse). The company currently uses a legacy point-of-sale system and spreadsheets for inventory. The CEO has asked you to recommend whether the company should adopt an AI-powered demand forecasting system to reduce stockouts and overstock.

title: Task Brief - Information Systems
text:
Your Task
Using the AI tool provided, develop a recommendation memo (300-500 words) that addresses:

Feasibility: Is an AI demand forecasting system appropriate for a company of this size and maturity? What data would they need, and do they likely have it?
Risks: What are the main risks of adopting this system? Consider data quality, staff capability, vendor lock-in, and cost.
Alternative: If AI forecasting is not the right first step, what should the company do instead?

title: Task Brief - Information Systems
text:
Instructions

You have 45 minutes to develop your recommendation using the AI chat tool.
Do not simply ask the AI to write the memo for you. Use the AI to explore the problem, test your reasoning, and refine your thinking.
Challenge the AI if something seems wrong or oversimplified. The AI may give plausible but incorrect or generic responses - it is your job to catch those.
Your final memo should reflect your own judgement informed by the conversation, not a copy of the AI’s output.

title: Task Brief - Information Systems
text:
What Makes a Good Response

Shows evidence of critical evaluation (not just accepting the AI’s first answer)
Considers the specific context (mid-sized Australian retail) rather than generic advice
Acknowledges trade-offs and limitations
Demonstrates iterative thinking - the recommendation evolved through conversation

title: Task Brief - Information Systems
text:
Sample Interaction Tasks (Alternative Scenarios)

Task 2: Cloud Migration Risk Assessment
A university department is considering migrating its student records system from on-premises servers to a cloud-based SaaS platform. Using the AI tool, develop a risk assessment that evaluates data sovereignty (Australian data must stay onshore), integration with existing systems, and the transition plan. Identify at least two risks the AI might understate or miss.


Task 3: AI Ethics Policy Review
A financial services company has drafted a one-page AI ethics policy. The policy states: “All AI decisions will be fair, transparent, and accountable.” Using the AI tool, critically evaluate this policy. Identify what is missing, what is unenforceable, and draft three specific, actionable clauses that would make the policy meaningful. Push back if the AI suggests vague improvements.

title: Exit Survey
text: The exit survey is administered at the end of each lab session via Qualtrics (Curtin institutional licence). Estimated completion time: 8-10 minutes.

title: Exit Survey
section: Why the Exit Survey is Needed
text:
Why the Exit Survey is Needed
The transcript data tells us what students did. The exit survey tells us three things transcripts cannot:

Confidence calibration - Do students who delegated to the AI believe their output is good? Comparing self-reported confidence (Q2) against task completion scores tests whether passive delegation produces unwarranted confidence in incorrect outputs. This is central to the misinformation risk argument: the harm of delegation is not just weaker output, but weaker output the student believes is strong.
Self-report vs observed behaviour gap - Do students know how they interacted? Comparing self-reported interaction style (Q5, Q6) against coded transcript behaviour reveals metacognitive awareness gaps. A student who reports “I challenged the AI throughout” but whose transcript shows predominantly delegation turns lacks awareness of their own AI interaction patterns. This gap is a finding in itself and an argument for structured scaffolding.
Baseline covariates - Prior AI experience (Q9, Q10) and formal AI instruction (Q11) provide covariates for the primary analysis. If random assignment produces groups that differ on prior experience, these allow statistical adjustment.
Condition awareness - Did nudge-group students notice the nudge (Q8a)? This distinguishes between automatic behavioural cueing and conscious reflection as the mechanism of action.

Without the survey, the study can show the nudge changed behaviour but cannot explain why or test whether the behavioural change was accompanied by appropriate confidence calibration.

title: Exit Survey
text:
Administration

Facilitator displays the Qualtrics link on the main screen at the end of the task
Students complete the survey independently and silently on the lab computer
Facilitator confirms all students have submitted before dismissing the session
RA downloads responses after each session and links to transcript data via session code

title: Exit Survey
text:
Survey Instrument


Section 1: Your Session Code
Q1. Please enter the session code shown on your screen.
[Text field]
This code links your survey responses to your conversation transcript. It does not identify you personally.



Section 2: Your Task
Q2. How confident are you that your final task output today was accurate and well-reasoned?
1 - Not confident at all 2 - Slightly confident 3 - Moderately confident 4 - Quite confident 5 - Very confident

Q3. How satisfied are you with the quality of your final task output?
1 - Very unsatisfied 2 - Unsatisfied 3 - Neutral 4 - Satisfied 5 - Very satisfied

Q4. At any point during the session, did something the AI said seem wrong, incomplete, or surprising to you?
Yes No Not sure
Q4a. (Shown only if Q4 = Yes) Briefly describe what seemed wrong or surprising.
[Text field - optional]



Section 3: How You Worked with the AI
Q5. Which of the following best describes how you worked with the AI tool today?
I mostly accepted what the AI said and used it to build my response I sometimes pushed back or asked follow-up questions, but mostly accepted the responses I regularly pushed back, questioned, or extended the AI responses I treated the AI as a thinking partner throughout - challenging it and building on its responses

Q6. How much did you feel like you were directing the conversation with the AI, versus following where it led?
1 - The AI mostly led, I followed 2 - The AI led more than I did 3 - About equal 4 - I led more than the AI 5 - I directed the conversation throughout

Q7. Did you feel the AI tool was helpful for completing the task today?

Q8. Did anything about the AI tool’s responses make you want to ask a follow-up question or push back?

Q8a. Did you notice anything unusual or different about the way the AI responded today (for example, any extra text or prompts in the responses)?
Yes No Not sure

[Text field - optional]



Section 4: Your Prior AI Experience
These questions help us understand the range of prior experience in the study. There are no right or wrong answers.
Q9. How often do you use AI tools (such as ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or similar) for study or work?

Q10. Before today, how would you describe your typical approach when using an AI tool for a task?

Q11. Have you received any formal instruction on how to use AI tools effectively (for example, in a class, workshop, or training session)?
Yes No Not sure



Section 5: About You
These questions are used only for statistical purposes to describe the study sample. Responses cannot be linked to your identity.
Q12. What is your current year of study?
First year Second year Third year Fourth year or above Postgraduate coursework Postgraduate research

Q13. What is your primary discipline or field of study?
[Dropdown: list of Curtin discipline areas]

Q14. Is there anything else you would like to tell us about your experience today?
[Text field - optional]

title: Exit Survey
text:
Analytic Notes
Confidence calibration analysis (within-unit only)
Q2 (confidence) is compared against the task completion score for the same student, within each unit separately. Task completion scores require discipline-specific judgement and are not comparable across units (a score of 4 in IS does not equal a score of 4 in Marketing). Within each unit: Spearman correlation between Q2 and task score; comparison of the confidence-performance gap between nudge and control groups; identification of the “overconfident delegator” pattern (high confidence + low task score + high proportion_delegation from transcript coding).
Self-reported versus observed interaction style
Q5 and Q6 (self-reported interaction style) are compared against the RA-coded behavioural dimensions for the same conversation. Divergences between self-report and observed behaviour are analytically interesting - a student who reports directing the conversation but whose transcript shows predominantly delegation turns may lack metacognitive awareness of their own AI interaction patterns.
Q4 (noticing errors) and the nudge effect
Q4 is the most direct self-report measure of whether the nudge is producing the intended friction. Nudge group students who report noticing something wrong and correcting it are exhibiting exactly the behaviour the intervention is designed to produce. This question should be analysed first when reviewing preliminary results.
Q8a (condition awareness) and mechanism interpretation
Q8a checks whether students in the nudge group consciously noticed the appended text. If nudged students show improved interaction behaviour without reporting awareness of the nudge, this supports an automatic behavioural cueing mechanism. If they noticed it and report it changed their behaviour, the mechanism is conscious reflection. Both are interesting; the distinction matters for generalisability and for designing future nudge variants.
Q10 as a baseline covariate
Q10 (prior AI approach) is included as a covariate in the mixed-effects model if nudge and control groups differ on this baseline measure. See coding_scheme.md Part 7 for the full model specification.

title: Task Brief - Marketing
text: You are a marketing consultant advising a small Australian skincare brand (“Banksia & Bloom”) that sells direct-to-consumer through its website and Instagram. The brand has 12,000 Instagram followers and an email list of 3,500. Revenue is $400K/year. The founder wants to expand into the retail channel (approaching Mecca or Priceline) and has asked you to assess whether the brand is ready and what the marketing implications would be.

title: Task Brief - Marketing
text:
Your Task
Using the AI tool provided, develop a marketing strategy assessment (300-500 words) that addresses:

Readiness assessment: Is Banksia & Bloom ready for retail? What does the brand need to have in place (brand positioning, pricing architecture, retail margins, packaging) before approaching a retailer?
Channel conflict: How should the brand manage the tension between its existing direct-to-consumer channel and a new retail partnership? What happens to pricing, exclusivity, and brand story?
Recommendation: Should they approach Mecca, Priceline, both, or neither right now? Justify with specific reasoning.

title: Task Brief - Marketing
text:
Instructions

You have 45 minutes to develop your assessment using the AI chat tool.
Do not simply ask the AI to write the assessment for you. Use the AI to explore options, test assumptions, and refine your thinking.
The AI may give generic marketing advice that does not account for the Australian retail landscape or the specific constraints of a small brand with limited resources. Challenge it on these points.
Your final assessment should reflect your own strategic judgement informed by the conversation.

title: Task Brief - Marketing
text:
What Makes a Good Response

Shows understanding of the specific Australian retail context (Mecca vs Priceline positioning, margin expectations)
Identifies realistic constraints for a small brand (cash flow, production capacity, negotiating power)
Demonstrates critical evaluation of the AI’s suggestions rather than accepting generic frameworks
Shows the thinking developed through iterative conversation

title: Task Brief - Marketing
text:
Sample Interaction Tasks (Alternative Scenarios)

Task 2: Social Media Crisis Response
Banksia & Bloom receives a viral negative review on TikTok (50K views) from an influencer claiming the product caused a skin reaction. The brand has not responded yet (it has been 6 hours). Using the AI tool, develop a response strategy covering: immediate actions (first 24 hours), communication to existing customers, and longer-term brand protection. Push back if the AI suggests overly cautious or overly aggressive approaches.


Task 3: Cross-Cultural Campaign Adaptation
Banksia & Bloom wants to expand into the Southeast Asian market, starting with Singapore and Malaysia. Using the AI tool, evaluate how the brand’s current messaging (“Australian native botanicals, clean beauty, minimalist luxury”) would need to adapt for these markets. Consider cultural values, beauty standards, competitive landscape, and regulatory differences. Challenge the AI if it makes broad generalisations about “Asian consumers.”

title: Literature Review
text:
Note



This is a working document. Sections marked [TO EXPAND] contain placeholder structure for additional literature. The existing references are drawn from the grant application; the review will be expanded during the writing phase (September-October 2026).

title: Literature Review
text:
AI in Higher Education Assessment
The integration of AI tools in higher education assessment is accelerating, but the pedagogical implications are unevenly understood. Kasneci et al. (2023) provide a broad survey of opportunities and challenges, identifying the tension between AI’s potential to support learning and its potential to undermine it when students disengage from the cognitive work that assessment is designed to elicit.
Stojanov, Gronbaek, and Kulahci (2024) move beyond the general framing to identify distinct student AI use patterns empirically. Their comparative study identifies a substantial subgroup of “assignment delegators” - students who use AI primarily to generate output rather than to support thinking. This is not a hypothetical risk; it is a measurable, persistent behaviour type. The delegator pattern is the specific problem the nudge intervention targets.
Mollick and Mollick (2023) approach the same problem from a design perspective, identifying seven approaches for integrating AI into student work. Their central argument - that the pedagogical challenge is keeping students as the active “human in the loop” - directly informs the Conversation, Not Delegation framework and the design of the nudge intervention.
[TO EXPAND: Additional sources on AI-integrated assessment design, student AI adoption patterns, and institutional policy responses.]

title: Literature Review
text:
The Delegation Problem
Passive delegation to AI carries a risk more serious than simply producing shallow work. When a student accepts a plausible but incorrect AI response without challenge, they have not just missed a learning opportunity - they have incorporated misinformation into their understanding with a degree of confidence they would not have from simply not knowing. In this sense, uncritical AI delegation may produce worse outcomes than no AI use at all.
This is the misinformation friction effect: the nudge is designed to create the friction that surfaces errors before they are accepted and acted upon. The argument is strongest in disciplines where factual accuracy, procedural correctness, or evidence-based reasoning are directly assessed.
[TO EXPAND: Literature on overreliance on AI, automation bias in decision-making, and the Dunning-Kruger intersection with AI-assisted work.]

title: Literature Review
text:
Scaffolding and Conversational Learning
Vygotsky’s (1978) scaffolding framework argues that structured support within a learner’s zone of proximal development enables capability development that does not occur without it. The nudge is a minimal scaffold: it does not provide the answer or direct the student’s response, but it makes the conversational path structurally easier to take.
Laurillard’s (2002) conversational framework extends scaffolding specifically to iterative dialogue as the mechanism of learning. The framework positions learning as a conversation between teacher and student (or, in this case, between student and AI) where meaning is constructed through successive cycles of articulation, re-articulation, and reflection. The nudge intervention targets the transition point in this cycle - the moment where a student decides whether to continue the conversation or accept and move on.
[TO EXPAND: Recent applications of Laurillard’s framework to AI-mediated learning. Scaffolding research in technology-enhanced learning environments.]

title: Literature Review
text:
Nudge Theory in Education
Thaler and Sunstein (2008) define a nudge as any aspect of choice architecture that alters people’s behaviour in a predictable way without forbidding any options or significantly changing their economic incentives. The conversational nudge applies this principle at the interface level: it does not require the student to push back, but it makes the pushback path cognitively cheaper by providing an explicit next action.
The application of nudge theory to educational contexts is well-established in areas such as enrolment, attendance, and financial aid uptake. Its application to in-task learning behaviour during AI interaction is less explored and represents a contribution of this study.
[TO EXPAND: Nudge interventions in higher education. Choice architecture in digital learning environments. Behavioural economics approaches to student engagement.]

title: Literature Review
text:
Guided vs Unguided AI Use
Recent empirical work confirms that structured engagement with AI consistently outperforms unstructured use. A study published in Frontiers in Psychology (2025) finds effects on task quality and academic engagement most pronounced when scaffolding is built into the task design rather than left to student initiative. This finding directly supports the design choice of embedding the nudge in the interface rather than relying on students to self-scaffold.
[TO EXPAND: Experimental studies comparing prompted vs unprompted AI interaction. Metacognitive scaffolding in AI-assisted learning.]

title: Literature Review
text:
Equity and Digital Literacy
The equity argument in this project is structural, not deficit-based. The evidence does not support the assumption that low SES or first-in-family students are inherently less capable of productive AI interaction. What the evidence does support is that students with fewer informal support networks outside the classroom have fewer opportunities to develop, or correct, their AI interaction habits through channels other than structured teaching.
A student who arrives with well-developed conversational AI habits has multiple pathways to refine those habits outside the classroom. A student without those networks is more dependent on the classroom being the place where scaffolding occurs. The nudge makes the classroom that place for every student; the equity analysis asks whether the benefit is differential.
[TO EXPAND: Literature on SES and digital literacy gaps. First-in-family student experiences with technology-enhanced learning. Structural vs deficit-based equity frameworks in higher education.]

title: Literature Review
text:
The Conversation, Not Delegation Framework
The Conversation, Not Delegation framework (Borck, 2026) names and operationalises the distinction between productive and unproductive AI interaction for the higher education teaching context. Conversation treats AI as a thinking partner: the student directs, challenges, and refines through iterative dialogue. Delegation treats AI as an answer machine: the student submits, accepts, and moves on.
The framework argues that the difference is not about tool choice or model quality - a student engaging conversationally with a less capable model may produce better learning outcomes than a student delegating passively to a frontier model. This observation motivates the planned follow-on study examining whether conversation quality can compensate for model quality.

title: Literature Review
text:
References
Borck, M. (2026). Conversation, Not Delegation. KDP. ISBN 979-8252824550.
Frontiers in Psychology. (2025). Optimizing academic engagement and mental health through AI: An experimental study on LLM integration in higher education. Frontiers in Psychology, 16. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2025.1641212
Kasneci, E., Sessler, K., Kuchemann, S., Bannert, M., Dementieva, D., Fischer, F., … and Kasneci, G. (2023). ChatGPT for good? On opportunities and challenges of large language models for education. Learning and Individual Differences, 103, 102274. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.lindif.2023.102274
Laurillard, D. (2002). Rethinking University Teaching: A Conversational Framework for the Effective Use of Learning Technologies (2nd ed.). RoutledgeFalmer.
Mollick, E. R., and Mollick, L. (2023). Assigning AI: Seven approaches for students, with prompts. The Wharton School Research Paper. https://ssrn.com/abstract=4475995

title: Closing the AI Literacy Gap
text:
Australian universities are embedding AI tools in assessment, but the skills required to use those tools effectively are not equally distributed. Students from low socioeconomic backgrounds and those who are first in their family to attend university have fewer informal support networks to develop productive AI interaction habits before they arrive. Without structured scaffolding, this access gap becomes a participation and attainment gap.
This project implements and evaluates a low-cost, scalable intervention: a conversational nudge appended to AI responses during structured lab sessions. The nudge makes the conversational path structurally easier, reducing the risk of uncritical acceptance at the point where it matters most. The equity analysis asks whether the benefit is uniform or differential - whether students who had fewer prior scaffolding opportunities gain more from the intervention.
The intervention is grounded in the Conversation, Not Delegation framework (Borck, 2026): the quality of AI-assisted work depends on whether the student is directing the AI through active conversation or passively accepting its output.
Funding: Curtin NBF Learning & Teaching Grant 2026 - AUD $20,000

title: Nudge Variants
text: To reduce habituation (students ignoring a repeated prompt), the nudge is not a single fixed line. Instead, one variant is randomly selected from the pool below and appended to each AI response in the nudge condition. All variants are designed to the same specification.

title: Nudge Variants
text:
Design Principles
Every variant must:

Invite pushback, not confirmation - the prompt should make it easier to disagree, not harder
Be domain-agnostic - work equally across Information Systems, Supply Chain, and Marketing
Assume no prior AI experience - a first-time AI user should understand the prompt
Make the conversational path easier, not mandatory - a suggestion, not an instruction
Be short - one sentence, scannable in under 3 seconds

title: Nudge Variants
text:
Variant Pool







#
Variant




1
Does that match what you expected? If something seems off, tell me what seems wrong.


2
Before you move on - does that actually answer your question, or just part of it?


3
Is there anything in that response that doesn’t quite fit with what you already know?


4
What would you challenge in that answer if you were reviewing someone else’s work?


5
Does that feel complete, or is there a gap worth pushing on?


6
If you had to argue the opposite, what would you say?


7
Is that specific enough to be useful, or is it still too general?


8
What’s the weakest part of that response?


9
Does that account for the details in your task, or is it a generic answer?


10
Would you trust that answer enough to use it without checking? Why or why not?

title: Nudge Variants
text:
Selection Method
One variant is selected uniformly at random per AI response. The same variant may appear consecutively by chance, but the probability decreases with pool size (1/10 = 10% chance of repeat). The selected variant is logged alongside the displayed response for analysis.

title: Nudge Variants
text:
Analysis Considerations

The primary analysis treats all nudge variants as equivalent (any nudge vs no nudge)
Exploratory analysis may examine whether specific variants are associated with higher challenge rates, though the study is not powered for variant-level comparisons
Variant #1 is the original wording described in the grant application and HREC documents

title: Nudge Variants
text:
Adding Variants
New variants can be added to the pool if they meet all five design principles above. Variants should be piloted with 2-3 students before being added to the live pool. The variant pool is defined in the application backend (keep-asking-app) and can be updated without changing the study design.

title: Coding Scheme
text:
This document defines the coding scheme for student-AI conversation transcripts collected during lab sessions. The scheme operates at two levels: automated measures computed directly from transcript logs, and human-coded behavioural measures applied by a trained Research Assistant (RA) and calibrated by co-investigators.
The scheme codes how students interact with the AI, not what they say about the topic. All behavioural dimensions are domain-agnostic and can be applied without disciplinary knowledge of Information Systems, Supply Chain Management, or Marketing.

title: Coding Scheme
text:
Unit of Analysis
The primary unit of analysis is the individual student turn - a single message sent by the student to the AI.
Turn 1 exclusion: The student’s first turn (the opening query) is excluded from behavioural coding. Turn 1 cannot be a follow-up, challenge, or extension because there is no prior AI response to react to. Including it would systematically inflate delegation rates and deflate engagement scores, especially for shorter conversations. Turn 1 is still counted in automated measures (turn count, word count) but is not coded on behavioural dimensions.
Coding begins at Turn 2 (the student’s first response after receiving an AI reply). Session-level behavioural measures are computed from Turn 2 onward.

title: Coding Scheme
text:
Part 1: Automated Measures
These measures are computed directly from the transcript log using scripts. No human coding is required.








Measure
Definition
Notes




turns_total
Total number of student turns in the conversation
Direct count from log (includes Turn 1)


turns_coded
Number of turns coded on behavioural dimensions
turns_total - 1 (excludes Turn 1)


words_total_student
Total words across all student turns
Computed from transcript text


words_mean_student
Average words per student turn
words_total_student / turns_total


words_mean_ai
Average words per AI response
Computed from transcript text


flesch_score_student
Flesch reading ease score across all student turns
Higher score = simpler language; lower = more complex


question_marks_total
Total question marks across all student turns
Crude proxy for follow-up questioning


question_marks_proportion
Proportion of turns containing at least one question mark
turns_with_question / turns_total


duration_minutes
Total session duration in minutes
From first to last timestamp if captured


response_latency_mean
Mean time between AI response and student reply in seconds
Requires timestamp logging; proxy for reflection time



Script location: scripts/compute_auto_measures.py

title: Coding Scheme
text:
Part 2: Human-Coded Behavioural Dimensions

Coded by: Research Assistant (RA)


Coverage: 100% of transcripts, Turn 2 onward
The RA applies three binary codes to every student turn from Turn 2 onward. Each code is Yes (1) or No (0).



Dimension 1: Follow-Up (FU)
Definition: Did the student ask a question or request that continues the conversation rather than ending it?
Code Yes (1) if: - The turn contains an explicit question directed at the AI - The turn requests clarification, elaboration, or more detail - The turn asks the AI to reconsider or try again
Code No (0) if: - The turn is a simple acknowledgement (“Thanks”, “Got it”, “Okay”) - The turn submits a new unrelated query without building on the previous response - The turn is the final message in the conversation with no question
Examples:








Turn
Code
Reason




“Can you explain why that approach would work better than the alternative?”
1
Explicit follow-up question


“What are the risks of that strategy?”
1
Continues the conversation


“Thanks, that is helpful.”
0
Acknowledgement, no follow-up


“Okay write me a summary of that.”
0
New directive, not a follow-up






Dimension 2: Challenge (CH)
Definition: Did the student explicitly push back on, express doubt about, or correct the AI response?
Code Yes (1) if: - The turn expresses disagreement with the AI’s response - The turn flags something as incorrect, incomplete, or implausible - The turn asks the AI to justify or defend its response - The turn notes a contradiction between the AI response and prior information
Code No (0) if: - The turn accepts the AI response without question - The turn asks for more information without expressing doubt - The turn rephrases the question without pushing back
Examples:








Turn
Code
Reason




“I don’t think that’s right - the case study said the timeline was six months, not three.”
1
Explicit correction


“That seems too simple. What are you not accounting for?”
1
Expresses doubt


“Can you tell me more about that?”
0
Request for more, no pushback


“Interesting, and what about the cost side?”
0
Extension without challenge






Dimension 3: Extension (EX)
Definition: Did the student introduce new information, a new constraint, or a new angle not already present in the AI response?
Code Yes (1) if: - The turn introduces a fact, constraint, or consideration not mentioned by the AI - The turn reframes the problem from a different perspective - The turn connects the AI response to an external concept or context - The turn adds a specific detail from the task brief that the AI did not reference
Code No (0) if: - The turn only references information already in the AI response - The turn asks for elaboration on something the AI already said - The turn is purely a follow-up with no new content
Examples:








Turn
Code
Reason




“What if the supplier is also a key customer - how does that change the analysis?”
1
New angle not in AI response


“The case said there is a 90-day lead time. Does that affect your recommendation?”
1
Introduces specific detail from task brief


“Can you expand on the second point?”
0
Elaboration only, no new content


“What are the advantages of option A?”
0
References AI content, no new information






Delegation (Derived in Analysis)
Delegation is not coded by the RA. It is derived automatically during analysis:
A turn is a delegation turn if: FU = 0 AND CH = 0 AND EX = 0
This derivation is deterministic and requires no judgement calls. Deriving it rather than coding it eliminates the possibility of contradictory data (e.g., a turn coded as delegation but also coded as having a follow-up). The RA’s task is simpler and the data is cleaner.
The delegation flag and all delegation-based session measures (proportion_delegation, delegation_streak_max) are computed by the analysis script from the three coded dimensions.



Turn-Level Scoring
Two scoring approaches are applied to each coded turn. Both are reported.
Unweighted score (equal dimensions):
turn_score_raw = FU + CH + EX







Score
Interpretation




0
Delegation - student accepted response without engagement


1
Minimal engagement - one dimension present


2
Moderate engagement - two dimensions present


3
Full engagement - follow-up, challenge, and extension all present



Weighted score (challenge-emphasised):
turn_score_weighted = FU + (2 x CH) + EX



Score
Interpretation




0
Delegation


1
Follow-up or extension only


2
Challenge alone, or follow-up + extension


3
Challenge + one other dimension


4
All three dimensions present



Rationale for dual scoring: The nudge explicitly invites pushback (“If something seems off, tell me what seems wrong”), making Challenge the dimension most directly targeted by the intervention. The weighted score tests whether the nudge effect is stronger when Challenge is given greater analytical weight. If the unweighted and weighted analyses converge, this confirms the finding is robust to scoring assumptions. If they diverge, it reveals whether the nudge primarily drives challenge behaviour (weighted effect larger) or broader engagement (similar effect sizes). Both outcomes are informative and both are reported.

title: Coding Scheme
text:
Part 3: Session-Level Rollup
Computed from turn-level codes after RA coding is complete. All behavioural measures exclude Turn 1.








Measure
Definition
Formula




mean_turn_score_raw
Average unweighted turn score
Mean of turn_score_raw (Turn 2+)


mean_turn_score_weighted
Average weighted turn score
Mean of turn_score_weighted (Turn 2+)


proportion_delegation
Proportion of coded turns that are delegation
Derived: turns where FU=0, CH=0, EX=0 / turns_coded


proportion_followup
Proportion of coded turns with follow-up
FU_turns / turns_coded


proportion_challenge
Proportion of coded turns with challenge
CH_turns / turns_coded


proportion_extension
Proportion of coded turns with extension
EX_turns / turns_coded


peak_turn_score_raw
Highest unweighted score in the session
Max of turn_score_raw


delegation_streak_max
Longest consecutive run of delegation turns
Sequence analysis on derived delegation flag (Turn 2+)



The proportion_challenge measure is expected to be the most sensitive indicator of the nudge effect, since the nudge explicitly invites pushback.

title: Coding Scheme
text:
Part 4: Task Completion Score

Coded by: Co-investigators (own unit transcripts only)


Coverage: 100% of transcripts within each co-investigator’s unit
This score requires disciplinary knowledge and is not coded by the RA.
Scale: 1 to 5







Score
Anchor




1
Student produced minimal output. Little evidence of engagement with the task requirements. Output largely repeats AI responses verbatim.


2
Student identified some relevant issues but analysis is incomplete or superficial. Limited evidence of independent reasoning beyond AI output.


3
Student addressed the main task requirements. Some evidence of critical evaluation. Output goes somewhat beyond AI responses but key gaps remain.


4
Student produced solid analysis addressing most task requirements. Clear evidence of critical thinking. Output integrates AI responses with student reasoning.


5
Student produced comprehensive, well-reasoned analysis. Strong evidence of critical evaluation and independent judgment. AI responses clearly interrogated and contextualised rather than accepted wholesale.



Important: Task completion scores are not comparable across units. A score of 4 in Information Systems does not represent the same absolute quality as a score of 4 in Marketing. The confidence calibration analysis (comparing self-reported confidence against task score) must be conducted within unit, not pooled across units. Cross-unit comparisons use the behavioural dimensions (FU, CH, EX) which are domain-agnostic.
Note: Each co-investigator should add 2-3 discipline-specific examples at each score level before coding their unit’s transcripts. These examples form the local calibration standard and should be documented in instruments/task_brief_[discipline].md.

title: Coding Scheme
text:
Part 5: Inter-Rater Reliability

Process

RA codes 100% of transcripts on dimensions FU, CH, and EX (Turn 2 onward)
Each co-investigator independently codes a 20-30% stratified sample from their own unit on the same dimensions
Cohen’s kappa is calculated per unit between RA codes and co-investigator codes
Lead investigator reviews all divergences
Divergences on behavioural dimensions below 0.70 kappa trigger scheme revision and recalibration
Divergences on contextual grounds (domain knowledge explaining the difference) are documented and reported as a finding rather than an error



Kappa Thresholds



Kappa
Interpretation
Action




< 0.50
Poor agreement
Stop, revise scheme, recode


0.50-0.69
Moderate agreement
Review divergences, minor revision


0.70-0.79
Acceptable agreement
Proceed, note limitations


0.80-0.89
Good agreement
Proceed


>= 0.90
Excellent agreement
Proceed





Calibration Sample Selection
The calibration sample should be stratified to include: - Equal proportions from nudge and control conditions - A spread across early, mid, and late sessions - Examples of both short (under 5 turns) and longer (over 15 turns) conversations

title: Coding Scheme
text:
Part 6: Coding Procedure

Before coding begins

RA attends a 1-hour briefing session with lead investigator
RA independently codes 10 practice transcripts from existing CloudCore data (Turn 2 onward only)
Lead investigator reviews practice codes and clarifies any ambiguous cases
RA codes a further 10 practice transcripts independently
Agreement check: if kappa on practice set is below 0.70, further calibration before proceeding to live data



During coding

RA skips Turn 1 and begins coding at Turn 2
RA codes each turn in order, top to bottom
RA does not revise earlier codes after reading later turns (each turn is coded in isolation)
RA flags any turn that is genuinely ambiguous using a comment field
Flagged turns are reviewed by lead investigator before final dataset is locked



After coding

Lead investigator checks flagged turns
Analysis script derives delegation flags and computes both unweighted and weighted scores
Co-investigators complete calibration sample coding
Kappa calculated and documented
Final coded dataset locked and version-controlled in data/processed/

title: Coding Scheme
text:
Part 7: Statistical Analysis

Primary analysis
The primary analysis uses a linear mixed-effects model to account for the nested structure of the data (students nested within units):
outcome ~ condition + (1 | unit)
Where: - outcome is the session-level measure (e.g., mean_turn_score_raw, proportion_challenge) - condition is the fixed effect (Nudge vs Control) - (1 | unit) is a random intercept for unit, accounting for the fact that students within the same unit share a task, a co-investigator, a discipline context, and potentially similar baseline characteristics
Why mixed-effects rather than simple ANOVA: Students are not independent across units. A student in Information Systems completes a different task, in a different discipline, with a different facilitator than a student in Marketing. Simple ANOVA treats all students as exchangeable and underestimates standard errors, inflating the risk of a false positive. The mixed-effects model partitions the variance: the fixed effect of condition (nudge vs control) is estimated after accounting for between-unit differences.
Covariates: Prior AI experience (Q10 from exit survey) is included as a fixed-effect covariate if nudge and control groups differ on this baseline measure:
outcome ~ condition + prior_ai_experience + (1 | unit)


Equity-disaggregated analysis
The equity analysis adds SES flag and first-in-family status as fixed effects and tests interactions:
outcome ~ condition * equity_flag + (1 | unit)
A significant interaction indicates the nudge effect differs between equity and non-equity cohorts (differential benefit). A non-significant interaction with a significant main effect indicates uniform benefit. Both findings are reported.


Confidence calibration analysis
The comparison of self-reported confidence (Q2) against task completion score is conducted within unit only. The task completion rubric requires discipline-specific judgement and scores are not comparable across units. Within each unit:

Spearman correlation between Q2 and task score
Comparison of the confidence-performance gap between nudge and control groups
Identification of the “overconfident delegator” pattern: high confidence + low task score + high proportion_delegation



Reporting
All analyses are reported with: - Effect sizes (Cohen’s d for group comparisons; marginal R-squared for mixed models) - 95% confidence intervals - Both unweighted and weighted scoring results - Equity-disaggregated results regardless of significance
Implementation: Analysis will use R with the lme4 package for mixed-effects models, or Python with statsmodels mixed linear models.

text:
Document History

title: Task Briefs - Retrieval-Framed Variants
text:
These tasks are designed to look like factual retrieval exercises but actually require the student to evaluate AI responses that may contain plausible errors. LLMs do not retrieve facts - they interpolate from training data. Tasks framed as retrieval (“What are the requirements for…?”, “List the key regulations…”) are where AI responses are most likely to be confidently wrong and where students are least likely to push back.
These tasks are not part of the primary study design. They are provided for: - Informal piloting during instrument development - A planned follow-on study comparing conversational vs retrieval-framed tasks - Demonstrating the task framing concept to co-investigators

title: Task Briefs - Retrieval-Framed Variants
text:
Design Rationale
The primary study tasks (see task_brief_information_systems.md, task_brief_supply_chain.md, task_brief_marketing.md) are conversational by design - they require iterative dialogue to develop a recommendation. There is no single right answer.
Retrieval-framed tasks differ in three ways: 1. They appear to have a factual answer - students expect the AI to “know” the answer 2. The AI will respond with high confidence - fluent, structured, authoritative tone 3. The response will likely contain errors - incorrect specifics, outdated information, fabricated details that sound plausible
This is where the nudge hypothesis is strongest: the nudge should interrupt the default acceptance pattern precisely when the AI’s confidence is highest and the student’s scepticism is lowest.

title: Task Briefs - Retrieval-Framed Variants
text:
Information Systems - Retrieval-Framed

Task: Australian Data Privacy Compliance Audit
You are preparing a compliance checklist for a small Australian fintech company that handles customer financial data. Using the AI tool, identify:

The specific requirements of the Australian Privacy Act 1988 that apply to a fintech handling customer transaction data. List the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) most relevant to this business and what each requires in practice.
The notification obligations under the Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme. What are the exact timeframes, who must be notified, and what are the penalties for non-compliance?
Any additional requirements specific to financial services data (e.g., APRA prudential standards, ASIC requirements).

What makes this a retrieval-framed task: Students will expect the AI to accurately cite specific legislation, timeframes, and penalty amounts. The AI will produce confident, structured responses - but may fabricate specific penalty figures, confuse APP numbers with their requirements, cite repealed provisions, or miss recent amendments. The student who accepts this without verification risks producing a compliance checklist that is plausibly structured but factually wrong.
Session length: 45 minutes.

title: Task Briefs - Retrieval-Framed Variants
text:
Supply Chain Management - Retrieval-Framed

Task: International Shipping Regulation Summary
A logistics company is planning to export temperature-controlled pharmaceutical products from Australia to three markets: Singapore, the UK, and the USA. Using the AI tool, compile:

The specific regulatory requirements for pharmaceutical cold chain exports from Australia. What are the TGA export requirements, and what documentation is needed?
The import requirements for each destination country. What certifications, inspections, or pre-approvals are required for temperature-sensitive pharmaceuticals entering Singapore (HSA), the UK (MHRA), and the USA (FDA)?
The shipping standards that apply. What are the GDP (Good Distribution Practice) requirements, and which specific temperature monitoring standards must be met?

What makes this a retrieval-framed task: Students will expect the AI to correctly name specific regulators, cite specific standards (GDP, WHO guidelines), and describe specific documentation requirements. The AI will produce detailed, authoritative-sounding responses - but may confuse TGA export permits with import requirements, cite outdated WHO technical supplements, fabricate specific temperature tolerance ranges, or describe requirements from the wrong regulatory jurisdiction. A student who delegates this task gets a professionally formatted but potentially dangerous compliance document.
Session length: 45 minutes.

title: Task Briefs - Retrieval-Framed Variants
text:
Marketing - Retrieval-Framed

Task: Australian Consumer Law and Advertising Compliance
A direct-to-consumer skincare brand is planning a marketing campaign that includes the following claims: “clinically proven to reduce wrinkles by 40%”, “dermatologist recommended”, and “Australia’s #1 natural skincare brand”. Using the AI tool, determine:

Which of these claims are permissible under Australian Consumer Law (ACL) and the Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) advertising requirements? For each claim, identify the specific legal standard that applies and what evidence would be required to substantiate it.
The penalties for misleading or deceptive conduct under the ACL. What are the current maximum penalties for corporations and individuals?
The complaint process. If a competitor lodges a complaint with Ad Standards or the ACCC, what is the process and what are the possible outcomes?

What makes this a retrieval-framed task: Students will expect the AI to correctly cite penalty amounts, name the correct regulatory bodies, and accurately describe the boundary between cosmetic claims (ACL) and therapeutic claims (TGA). The AI will produce confident legal-sounding analysis - but may cite outdated penalty figures, confuse the roles of Ad Standards, the ACCC, and the TGA, misstate the therapeutic/cosmetic boundary, or fabricate case precedents. A student who delegates this task produces advice that sounds legally informed but may be substantively wrong on the specific points that matter most.
Session length: 45 minutes.

title: Task Briefs - Retrieval-Framed Variants
text:
Analytic Note
If these tasks are used in a follow-on study, the key comparison is:




Conversational task
Retrieval-framed task




Nudge
Nudge + conversational
Nudge + retrieval


Control
Control + conversational
Control + retrieval



The hypothesis is that the nudge effect (nudge - control difference) will be larger for retrieval-framed tasks than for conversational tasks, because retrieval-framed tasks produce the highest-confidence AI responses and the lowest baseline student scepticism.

title: Task Brief - Supply Chain Management
text: You are a supply chain analyst for an Australian food manufacturer that produces chilled ready meals. The company sources ingredients from 15 domestic suppliers and 4 international suppliers (New Zealand, Thailand, Italy, USA). Recent disruptions - a shipping delay from Thailand and a drought affecting a key domestic vegetable supplier - have exposed vulnerabilities in the supply chain. Management wants a plan.

title: Task Brief - Supply Chain Management
text:
Your Task
Using the AI tool provided, develop a supply chain resilience briefing (300-500 words) that addresses:

Vulnerability assessment: Which parts of this supply chain are most vulnerable and why? Consider lead times, substitutability, perishability, and geographic concentration.
Mitigation strategies: Recommend two to three specific actions the company should take in the next 6 months. Be concrete - “diversify suppliers” is not enough; specify how and at what cost.
Trade-offs: What are the costs or downsides of your recommended strategies? Who bears them?

title: Task Brief - Supply Chain Management
text:
Instructions

You have 45 minutes to develop your briefing using the AI chat tool.
Do not simply ask the AI to write the briefing for you. Use the AI to explore the problem, test scenarios, and pressure-test your reasoning.
The AI may give textbook answers that do not account for the specific constraints of a chilled food supply chain (perishability, cold chain, regulatory requirements). It is your job to catch these gaps.
Your final briefing should reflect your own analysis informed by the conversation.

title: Task Brief - Supply Chain Management
text:
What Makes a Good Response

Demonstrates understanding of supply chain-specific constraints (not generic risk management)
Identifies something the AI missed or oversimplified
Quantifies or concretises where possible (lead times, costs, timelines)
Shows the reasoning evolved through iterative conversation

title: Task Brief - Supply Chain Management
text:
Sample Interaction Tasks (Alternative Scenarios)

Task 2: Last-Mile Delivery Optimisation
An Australian e-commerce company delivers to both metropolitan and regional areas. Metropolitan deliveries are profitable; regional deliveries lose money on average. Using the AI tool, analyse the trade-offs between three options: (a) increase regional delivery charges, (b) partner with Australia Post for regional, (c) establish regional micro-fulfilment centres. Challenge the AI on assumptions about Australian geography and logistics costs.


Task 3: Supplier Selection Under Uncertainty
A manufacturer needs to choose between two suppliers for a critical component. Supplier A is cheaper but based overseas with a 6-week lead time. Supplier B is 20% more expensive but domestic with a 1-week lead time. Annual demand is uncertain (could vary by 30%). Using the AI tool, develop a recommendation that accounts for total cost of ownership, risk, and demand variability. Push back if the AI gives a simple cost comparison.

title: HREC Application
text:
1) Plain Language Summary (100-200 words)
This study evaluates a simple interface prompt (“nudge”) that encourages students to challenge and continue the conversation with an AI tool during structured lab sessions. Students from multiple units complete a short, discipline-relevant task using a supplied AI chat interface. Half of students (randomised) will see a one-line nudge appended to each AI reply:

“Does that match what you expected? If something seems off, tell me what seems wrong.”

The other half will not. We will compare interaction quality (e.g., number and nature of follow-ups) and task outcomes across groups. We will also examine equity-disaggregated outcomes (low SES, first-in-family) using de-identified enrolment data matched to session records by Curtin’s analytics team. No personal identifiers are collected by the research team; participation is voluntary and does not affect grades. Results will inform equitable AI-integrated assessment at Curtin and will be shared as open resources and a peer-reviewed paper.

title: HREC Application
text:
2) Aims and Significance
Primary aim: Test whether a minimal conversational nudge increases critical, iterative student-AI interaction and improves task outcomes in structured lab sessions.
Equity question: Determine whether benefits are uniform or differential for low SES and first-in-family students.
Significance: Addresses structural disadvantage in AI-integrated assessment by providing in-class scaffolding that may substitute for unequal access to informal AI support networks outside class. Produces equity-disaggregated evidence for Curtin’s NBF reporting.

title: HREC Application
text:
3) Background and Rationale
The project is grounded in the Conversation, Not Delegation framework: students who guide and challenge AI through iterative dialogue learn and perform better than those who passively accept plausible but incorrect outputs. Uncritical delegation risks worse outcomes than no AI use at all. The nudge reduces the cognitive cost of continuing the conversation at the exact point a student might otherwise accept a first reply. Prior work and theory support scaffolding and conversational models of learning; this project operationalises those principles in the interface. Infrastructure and prior design experience (CloudCore, LocoLLM, keep-asking) allow controlled session delivery and automatic conversation logging without personal identifiers.

title: HREC Application
text:
4) Methods

4.1 Design
Randomised controlled lab sessions within multiple units; individual randomisation to Nudge vs Control. All students receive the same task and AI model settings (per condition).
Exploratory model comparison: Some sessions use a local small model (Configuration A) vs a frontier model via API (Configuration B). Model comparison is descriptive only.


4.2 Setting and Procedure
1-2 sessions per participating unit (3 or more units across disciplines). Each session uses lab computers provisioned by the team; no personal accounts are required; the environment is zero-cost to students.
Students complete a discipline-relevant task that requires iterative AI interaction; conversations are logged automatically. A 10-minute exit survey follows.
Nudge wording (Nudge condition only): “Does that match what you expected? If something seems off, tell me what seems wrong.”


4.3 Participants and Recruitment
Population: Curtin students enrolled in participating units (ISYS6020 + at least two additional units in Marketing and Supply Chain; final list TBC). Participation is voluntary and does not affect grades. Sessions are scheduled at varied times to support students with work/caring commitments.
Target n: ~60-120 participants total (20-30 per session across 3 or more units). A power analysis will be finalised once units and cohort sizes are confirmed.
Recruitment via PI and co-investigators with plain-language invitations on LMS and email. We will encourage participation from low SES / first-in-family students (equity disaggregation happens at analysis stage; no differential treatment during sessions).
Inclusion: Currently enrolled students in participating units; aged 18+; able to attend scheduled lab sessions.
Exclusion: Students under 18; students who do not consent or do not attend lab sessions; students without capacity to consent.


4.4 Data Collected

Student number: Collected at session login for equity linkage only. Stored in a temporary linkage table separate from research data. Destroyed after de-identification (see Section 8).
Conversation transcripts: Tagged with session code only (no student number). Logged automatically.
Exit survey responses via Qualtrics (Curtin institutional licence): self-report of confidence, prior AI experience, demographics.
Task outputs for rubric scoring.
Equity flags (low SES, first-in-family): matched to session codes via linkage table, then de-identified. The research team analyses only the de-identified dataset.



4.5 Measures

Conversation depth: turn count per session
Follow-up quality: behavioural coding (specificity, criticality, challenge)
Task completion quality: rubric scores
Confidence calibration: self-reported confidence vs performance

Analyses: Linear mixed-effects models (condition as fixed effect, unit as random intercept) to account for nesting of students within units; effect sizes; equity-disaggregated subgroup analyses with interaction terms. Confidence calibration conducted within-unit only. Inter-rater reliability (Cohen’s kappa) between RA and unit co-investigators on calibration samples; kappa < 0.70 triggers scheme refinement before full analysis.

title: HREC Application
text:
5) Risks and Mitigation (Low-Risk Justification)
Physical risk: None.
Psychological risk: Minimal; students may feel minor performance anxiety. Mitigation: clear voluntariness, short tasks, supportive facilitation, option to skip any question and withdraw at any time without consequence.
Privacy risk: Low; student numbers are collected at login for equity linkage only, stored in a temporary linkage table, and destroyed after de-identification. All research data (transcripts, surveys, task outputs) use session codes only. The research team analyses a fully de-identified dataset.
Academic risk / power relationship: Participation does not affect unit grades; sessions are optional; non-teaching facilitators assist where feasible; consent taken outside assessment contexts; a no-penalty alternative is available (e.g., self-paced practice task not logged).
Data security: University infrastructure; controlled folders; role-based access; encryption at rest; retention and destruction schedule below.

title: HREC Application
text:
6) Benefits
Direct to participants: Opportunity to learn effective AI interaction habits and receive structured feedback.
To Curtin: Equity-disaggregated evidence to inform AI-integrated assessment policy and NBF reporting. Open-access resource pack and ACIS 2026 dissemination.

title: HREC Application
text:
7) Consent (Digital Only)
Type: Opt-in written consent (digital tick-box or signed paper).
Process: Participants receive a Participant Information Sheet (PIS) at least 48 hours prior (via LMS/email). Consent is captured before the session; students may withdraw anytime before data aggregation by quoting their session code.
No deception. No concealment of study aims; the nudge wording is transparently described in PIS.

title: HREC Application
text:
8) Privacy, Confidentiality and Data Security
Data flow and de-identification process:

Student logs into the lab interface using their student number. The system assigns a random session code and records a temporary linkage (student number to session code) in a restricted-access linkage table.
All research data (conversation transcripts, task outputs, Qualtrics exit survey responses) are tagged with the session code only. Student numbers do not appear in research data.
After data collection closes, the PI uses the linkage table to match equity flags (low SES, first-in-family status) from Curtin SIMS to session codes.
A de-identification script strips student numbers from the dataset, retaining only session codes and equity flags. The linkage table is permanently destroyed. Destruction is logged and auditable.
The research team receives and analyses a fully de-identified dataset. No pathway exists to re-identify participants after this step.

Storage: Curtin network drive / SharePoint with role-based access for PI, approved co-investigators, and RA; versioning; backups per university policy.
Retention and destruction: 5 years post-publication (or per Curtin RDM policy for L&T research), then secure deletion (cryptographic erase) of raw datasets and transcripts; anonymised analysis scripts can be retained in a public repository with no raw transcripts.

title: HREC Application
text:
9) Use of AI / External Services
Frontier model via API: The team funds API use from the internal grant; the interface does not store personal data; prompts and responses are session-coded only.
Content filtering: The interface prevents entering personal health/financial information and reminds students not to include PII.
Data residency: No PII; content pertains to course tasks; API calls are limited to session content and excluded from provider training where setting is available.

title: HREC Application
text:
10) Incentives
None offered. Sessions are designed to be short and scheduled at varied times to maximise accessibility.

title: HREC Application
text:
11) Funding, Conflicts, Monitoring and Timeline
Funding: Curtin NBF Learning & Teaching Grant 2026 (internal). Budget supports RA, API costs, instrument development, session delivery, resource production, and ACIS 2026 dissemination.
COIs: None declared. The PI’s authorship of Conversation, Not Delegation is disclosed as scholarly background; no commercial interest.
Monitoring: PI reviews session logs for anomalies weekly during data collection; any adverse incidents (e.g., distress) trigger immediate pause and referral to student support services.
Complaints: PIS lists Curtin HREC contact for participants to lodge concerns independent of the research team.
Protocol deviations: Documented and reported with corrective actions to HREC if materially affecting risk.

12) Dissemination
Open-access resource pack (facilitation guide, nudge variants, task design principles, exit survey instrument) on Curtin repository and The AI Exchange. ACIS 2026 paper submission. Equity-disaggregated findings included in NBF reporting.

title: HREC Application
text:
13) Vulnerable Populations
The study involves adult university students. Power imbalances are mitigated via voluntariness, non-assessment, neutral facilitation, and alternatives.
Equity cohorts (low SES; first-in-family) are not treated differently in sessions; equity analysis occurs post hoc using de-identified cohort flags to examine whether benefits are uniform or differential.

title: HREC Application

14) Publications
Peer-reviewed: Targeting ACIS 2026; anonymised transcript excerpts only; no identifiers.
Open resources: Facilitation guide, nudge variants, task design principles, exit survey instrument (no raw data).

Captures confidence calibration (self-reported confidence vs task performance, analysed within-unit), self-reported interaction style (compared against coded transcript data), prior AI experience (covariate), and condition awareness (whether nudge-group students noticed the nudge).


H. Coding Scheme (Summary)
(See separate file: instruments/coding_scheme.md)
Three coded dimensions (binary, Turn 2 onward):

Follow-Up (FU): did the student ask a follow-up question?
Challenge (CH): did the student push back on or correct the AI?
Extension (EX): did the student introduce new information or a new angle?

Delegation is derived in analysis (FU=0, CH=0, EX=0), not coded separately. Results reported using both unweighted (FU+CH+EX) and challenge-weighted (FU+2xCH+EX) scoring.
Reliability: RA codes 100%; each co-I codes 20-30% from their unit; Cohen’s kappa per unit; kappa < 0.70 triggers scheme refinement.
